sections.content:
Looking at the red dots, I can see two cross patterns:
1. Top-right cross: nodes 3, 5, 7, 8 at positions (9,52), (10,51), (10,53), (11,52) - center would be at (10, 52)
2. Bottom-left cross: nodes 24, 25, 27, 28 at positions (51,10), (52,9), (52,11), (53,10) - center would be at (52, 10)
But wait, node 4 is at (10, 28) and node 17 is at (28, 10) - these are isolated red dots, not part of crosses.
So we have:
- Two cross patterns (X-shaped, 4 pixels each since diagonals aren't 4-connected)
- Two isolated red dots at (10, 28) and (28, 10)
The bars are at minimum. Maybe I need to click on the red cross patterns or the isolated red dots to complete the level. Let me try clicking on one of the cross centers or isolated red dots.
Actually, looking at the earlier history, I clicked on (10, 31) which was described as a "red dot" but that's not in the current list. Let me check what node 4 at (10, 28) might be - it's close to where the green bar is.
Wait, let me reconsider. The green bar is node 2 with 5 pixels. Let me check its boundary.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check bar positions
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] in (2, 16):  # green and yellow bars
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the isolated red dots
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] in (4, 17):
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the cross centers
print("\nCross 1 (top-right) center approx: (10, 52)")
print("Cross 2 (bottom-left) center approx: (52, 10)")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
